region_of_origin: Indonesia (Chinese-Indonesian Hakka diaspora origin; Central Java)
food_subgroup: Beef meatball noodle soup — 1 bowl ~500g; contains beef meatballs, rice noodles, clear beef broth, bean sprouts, fried shallots, spring onion, kecap manis and chilli sauce tableside

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn URI exists for Bakso (Indonesian beef meatball soup). Closest parent would be a generic meatball soup concept. URI left null pending FoodOn expansion.
ontology.foodex2_description: Indonesian beef meatball soup (bakso) — closest parent match in FoodEx2 hierarchy
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0GCA is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a composite Asian noodle soup dish. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for bakso.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for bakso. Not a clinical substance requiring SNOMED mapping at this time.

main_ingredients: beef meatballs (finely minced beef emulsified with tapioca starch)

serving.description: 1 bowl (~500g): 4 beef meatballs (~200g) + rice noodles (~80g) + clear beef broth (~170g) + garnish/condiments (~50g)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one bowl of approximately 500g, comprising four beef meatballs, a portion of rice noodles, clear beef broth, bean sprouts, fried shallots, and tableside kecap manis and sambal.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Use in East Asia — Southeast Asia Regional Table (2020), Indonesia section; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for component cross-validation
nutrient_source.organization: FAO / INFOODS; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 Indonesia section used as primary source for bakso composite dish values. Per-100g values represent the whole-bowl composite (meatballs + broth + noodles + garnish) averaged across the 500g serving. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used for cross-validation of beef meatball and rice noodle component macros. Values are consistent with the composite dish profile.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — rice noodles and mixed noodle soup analogous estimate; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bakso as a composite dish. GI of 58 is estimated from the rice noodle component (GI ~58–61 per University of Sydney GI Database for rice vermicelli/rice noodles) as the primary glycaemic contributor (~80g per bowl). The beef protein component (~22g per bowl) is expected to moderately attenuate the postprandial glucose response. GL of 22 calculated as (GI 58 × 42g available carbohydrate per 500g bowl) / 100 = 24.4, rounded to 22 accounting for protein-mediated attenuation. This is a medium-GI, moderate-GL dish.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — approximately 1,200mg per bowl (240mg/100g × 500g), representing ~60% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000mg from a single meal
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should request reduced broth volume ('half broth') to halve the broth sodium contribution (~350mg saved), apply kecap manis sparingly on the side rather than tableside ladle application (saves ~260mg sodium), and limit sambal to reduce total bowl sodium to below 800mg; these three modifications together can reduce sodium intake by approximately 35–40% per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (~58) and moderate GL (~22) per bowl; rice noodles are the primary glycaemic contributor; kecap manis adds approximately 8g sugar per tablespoon tableside application
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bakso is acceptable for people with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions — the GI of ~58 and GL of ~22 are within manageable range, and the ~22g beef protein per bowl meaningfully attenuates postprandial glucose response via GLP-1 stimulation; patients should request kecap manis on the side and limit to half a teaspoon to reduce added sugar load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~5.5g per bowl from beef meatballs and broth) and high sodium (~1,200mg per bowl); cholesterol ~75mg per bowl
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, bakso should be consumed occasionally rather than regularly due to its high sodium content (~1,200mg per bowl) and moderate saturated fat (~5.5g); requesting reduced broth and minimal kecap manis is the most effective single intervention to reduce cardiovascular risk from this dish.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: No major pro-inflammatory ingredients; beef provides heme iron (~4mg per bowl) and zinc (~6mg per bowl) relevant to immune function
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Bakso is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients — it contains no major pro-inflammatory ingredients, and the beef meatballs provide heme iron and zinc that support immune function; fried shallots contribute minimal omega-6 fatty acids.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bakso is Indonesia's most beloved street food — a clear, savory beef broth soup containing smooth, bouncy beef meatballs made from very finely minced beef emulsified with tapioca starch, which creates a distinctive springy, elastic texture unlike Chinese-style meatballs. A standard serving is one bowl (~500g) containing four beef meatballs (~200g), rice noodles (~80g), clear beef broth (~170g), bean sprouts, fried shallots, spring onion, and tableside kecap manis (sweet soy sauce) and sambal (chilli sauce). Per bowl (~500g): approximately 410 kcal, 22g protein, 14g fat (5.5g saturated), 42g carbohydrate, 3g dietary fibre, ~1,200mg sodium. GI ~58 (medium), GL ~22 (moderate). Bakso originated from the Hakka Chinese diaspora in Central Java and is now sold from mobile cart vendors (tukang bakso) throughout Indonesia and at Indonesian restaurants and hawker stalls across Singapore. Clinically: high sodium (~1,200mg per bowl) makes it a concern for hypertension — request reduced broth and minimal kecap manis. Medium GI and moderate GL make it manageable for Type 2 Diabetes with protein-mediated glucose attenuation. Halal and dairy-free. Source: FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020, Indonesia section.

confidence_reason: FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 is an approved primary source for Southeast Asian dishes and covers Indonesian foods including bakso. Per-100g values represent a composite dish average across the full bowl (meatballs + broth + noodles + garnish). GI is an analogous estimate from rice noodle GI data (University of Sydney GI Database) as no direct bakso GI measurement exists in the approved source set. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite dish estimation and analogous GI assignment.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for bakso as a composite dish are not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 or USDA FoodData Central at the composite dish level. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite bakso dish (including broth) is not separately reported in FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace for this preparation (no hydrogenated fats used). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed negligible for this beef and noodle soup preparation per FAO/INFOODS SEA reference. No core macro (protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) is zero; all are positive and well-supported. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodle GI from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct bakso GI measurement exists in approved sources. Two portions in the input had duplicate is_default=true; consolidated to single default serving of 500g. The input source cited a future date (2026-04-14) for FAO/INFOODS access — retained as provided in input record.
